RampNet · internal verification · 2026-07-02

Did we really over-count? A re-verification of the published evaluation protocol

Requested by Jon: “Are you absolutely certain there was an error in the original paper and our experimental approach? Double- and triple-check, produce visual examples of what we double-counted, and explain in greater detail.”

Verdict: the over-counting is real, confirmed three independent ways — but the framing needs one important refinement. The Stage 2 evaluator does exactly what Section 3.3 of the paper says it does. This is not a hidden code bug that silently diverged from the stated method; it is a stated protocol that differs from standard detection evaluation in two ways, both of which bias precision and recall upward. Separately, there is a genuine internal inconsistency: the Stage 1 evaluator implements a different (stricter) matching rule than the one the paper describes and Stage 2 uses, so the paper's Stage-1-vs-Stage-2 comparison mixed two protocols.

Evidence 1
The exact code at tag v1.0-iccv2025, executed line-for-line on constructed and real inputs (§2–§3).
Evidence 2
Real gold-set panoramas: the released model + released weights produce detections that the published matcher counts twice. Found on 5 of 5 panoramas tested (§4).
Evidence 3
The Hyak re-evaluation on all 1,000 gold panoramas: swapping only the matching rule on identical heatmaps moves precision −1.0 pt and recall −4.4 pts at the published operating point (§6).
Retraction
One claim in my earlier issue draft is wrong and I withdraw it: the matching region is a true circle, not an ellipse (§7).

1What the paper says, in its own words

Section 3.3 (“Correctness metrics”) defines the protocol used for both stages. The two highlighted sentences are the whole story:

“For each predicted label, we check if there are any ground-truth points within an 88-pixel radius in the 4096 × 2048 pixel panorama. If not, we mark the label as a false positive. Conversely, if it does match one or more ground-truth points, those within the radius will be counted as a true positives. If more than one predicted label matches the same ground-truth point, we pick the one with highest confidence and ignore the others entirely in our calculation. This is to ensure that no ground-truth points are counted more than once. If any of the ground-truth points are not detected, we count these as false negatives.” RampNet, ICCV'25 CV4A11y workshop, §3.3

Standard practice in detection evaluation (PASCAL VOC, COCO, and keypoint benchmarks alike) enforces one-to-one matching: each detection may claim at most one ground-truth object, and a detection whose only nearby ground truth is already claimed counts as a false positive. The paper's protocol departs from this twice, and both departures push the numbers up:

Departure A (green) — one detection may count as several true positives. If a single predicted point lies within the radius of two labeled curb ramps, it contributes two TP entries. Recall credit is given for a ramp that no detection individually found, and both TP entries also enter the precision numerator.

Departure B (red) — redundant detections vanish instead of counting against precision. A second detection of an already-claimed ramp is “ignored entirely”: it appears in neither the TP nor the FP column. Under VOC/COCO rules it would be a false positive.

Note what this means for the erratum: the sentence “this is to ensure that no ground-truth points are counted more than once” is satisfied — no ground-truth point is double-counted. What gets double-counted is the detection: one predicted point is entered into the score ledger twice.

2The code, as published

This is the matching loop from stage_two/evaluate.py at tag v1.0-iccv2025 (lines 417–433), the code that produced the paper's model results. Green is departure A; red is departure B.

current_image_preds_sorted_by_conf = sorted(pred_peaks_normalized, key=..., reverse=True)
k_gt_mappings = [False] * len(gt_points_normalized)
for pred_x_norm, pred_y_norm, pred_conf in current_image_preds_sorted_by_conf:
    ...
    passes = False
    for k_gt, (gt_x_norm, gt_y_norm) in enumerate(gt_points_normalized):
        ...
        if dist_sq < RADIUS_THRESHOLD_PIXELS_SQ:
            passes = True
            if not k_gt_mappings[k_gt]:                      # A: no `break` — the SAME prediction
                all_pred_details_for_ap.append((pred_conf, True))  # appends one TP per nearby unclaimed GT
            k_gt_mappings[k_gt] = True
    if not passes:                                           # B: a prediction whose only in-radius GTs
        all_pred_details_for_ap.append((pred_conf, False))   # are claimed sets passes=True above and
                                                             # appends NOTHING — it is never a FP

Faithfulness check against §3.3: departure A implements “those within the radius will be counted as true positives” (plural, per prediction); departure B implements “ignore the others entirely.” Since predictions are processed in descending confidence order, each ground truth is claimed by the highest-confidence prediction, exactly as described. Stage 2 code and paper text agree.

Where they disagree is Stage 1. The dataset evaluator (stage_one/dataset_evaluation/evaluate.py:144–163 at the tag) breaks after claiming one ground truth — one-to-one on the TP side — and routes redundant matches to a separate “ignored” counter rather than FP. So the paper's headline Stage 1 numbers (94.0% precision / 92.5% recall) were computed under a stricter matching rule than the Stage 2 numbers they are directly compared against in §4.3 (“93.9% precision at the same recall threshold”). That comparison mixed two protocols. And the crop-model evaluator (stage_one/crop_model/ps_and_manual_model/evaluate.py:365–385) contains the identical no-break pattern as Stage 2 — my earlier issue draft understated this as “duplicate-drop only.”

3Executable demonstration, using the published code verbatim

I ported the loop above character-for-character (same variable names, same coordinate scaling, radius 0.022 × 1024 = 22.53 heatmap px) and ran it next to a standard greedy one-to-one matcher. Scenario C is one panorama containing both failure modes plus one honest false positive — the same four detections, scored two ways:

Published matcher

pred#0 (conf 0.98) within r of GT#0 -> append TP
pred#0 (conf 0.98) within r of GT#1 -> append TP
pred#1 (conf 0.90) within r of GT#2 -> append TP
pred#2 (conf 0.70) matches no GT    -> append FP
pred#3 (conf 0.62) within r of GT#2
        (already claimed)           -> append NOTHING
ledger: TP=3 FP=1  →  P=0.75  R=3/3=1.00

Standard one-to-one

pred#0 (conf 0.98) claims GT#0      -> TP
pred#1 (conf 0.90) claims GT#2      -> TP
pred#2 (conf 0.70) no GT in radius  -> FP
pred#3 (conf 0.62) duplicate of an
        already-claimed GT          -> FP

ledger: TP=2 FP=2  →  P=0.50  R=2/3=0.67

Four detections went in; the published matcher's ledger contains three TPs (two of them from the single detection pred#0) and only one FP (the duplicate evaporated). Precision 0.75 vs 0.50, recall 1.00 vs 0.67, on identical model output. The magnitude here is exaggerated by the tiny example, but the direction is mechanical: the published protocol can only ever raise P and R relative to one-to-one, never lower them.

4Real examples: this is what we double-counted

These are actual gold-set panoramas, the actual released model weights (projectsidewalk/rampnet-model), the paper's exact test-time augmentation and peak extraction (min_distance=10, confidence ≥ 0.55), matched against the repo's own manual_labels/*.txt. Every panorama I tested (5 of 5, chosen because their labels contain close pairs) produced at least one double-counted detection. Top panel: image with annotations. Bottom panel: the model's heatmap — one merged peak where there are two labeled ramps.

 ground-truth curb ramp (manual label)  90-px matching radius around each GT  model detection (predicted point)

These aren't cherry-picked failures of the model — in every image the model did something reasonable (it found the ramp cluster). They are failures of the ledger: the score sheet credits one point of model output with two rows of true positives.

5How often can this fire? Structurally: a lot

Departure A needs two ground-truth ramps within 2r ≈ 180 panorama px of each other (so one point can sit inside both radii). Curb ramps come in adjacent pairs by construction — dual-ramp corners, islands, medians. Measured over the repo's 1,000 gold-label files:

Geometry in the 3,919-point gold setcount
GT pairs closer than 2r (one detection can claim both)816 pairs
Panoramas containing at least one such pair418 / 1,000
GT points involved in at least one such pair1,613 (41.2%)
GT pairs closer than r (a detection on one ramp also claims the other)234 pairs

Whether a site actually fires depends on the model producing one merged peak instead of two. That is exactly what the pipeline encourages at close range: training targets are Gaussians with σ = 10 heatmap px, so two ramps ~40–90 panorama px apart produce overlapping blobs, and peak extraction suppresses secondary peaks within min_distance = 10 heatmap px (40 pano px). The aggregate numbers below confirm it fires at scale.

6Aggregate impact, measured on Hyak (all 1,000 panos)

From the re-evaluation you ran on klone (A40, released HF weights, TTA, r = 0.022, operating point conf ≥ 0.55). The middle row is the controlled experiment: identical heatmaps, only the matching rule changed.

ConfigurationPrecisionRecallNote
Published (paper env + paper matcher, committed results)0.9380.935README / paper operating point
Re-run heatmaps + paper matcher0.9590.917drift vs published: env, JPEG re-encode
Re-run heatmaps + one-to-one matcher0.9490.873corrected protocol
Isolated effect (same heatmaps, matcher swap only)Δ
Precision (duplicates become FPs)−1.0 pt
Recall (each detection may claim one ramp)−4.4 pts

A −4.4 pt recall delta over 3,919 GT points ≈ 170+ ground-truth ramps whose “detections” were credit from a neighbor's peak. Corrected end-to-end numbers at the published operating point: P 0.949 / R 0.873, with interpolated AP 0.9205 (vs the published 0.9236, which was raw, uninterpolated AP over the inflated ledger — the two AP figures also differ in interpolation convention, so quote them side by side with that caveat).

What survives untouched: the paper's core claims. The dataset-generation pipeline, the model, and the comparison against Weld et al. (0.38 AP, evaluated under the same protocol — internally consistent) are unaffected. Corrected RampNet numbers remain far beyond prior work and near the Stage 1 dataset's own agreement level. This is a metrics-accounting erratum, not a results retraction.

7Corrections to my own earlier claims

You asked me to triple-check; the check cut both ways. Three things in the drafted issue #9 / README erratum need fixing before anything is posted:

Earlier claimVerdict on re-check
“The matching radius is effectively an ellipse, 2× more permissive vertically.” Wrong — withdraw. The heatmap (1024×512) is exactly ¼ of the panorama (4096×2048) in both axes, so distance in heatmap px is isotropic. The matching region is a true circle of radius 90.1 pano px. (Only footnote-worthy: the paper says “88-pixel radius”; the code's 0.022 × 4096 = 90.1 px.)
“The crop-model evaluator has the same duplicate-drop pattern.” Understated. It has the identical full pattern — no break, so multi-TP double-counting too.
Framing the Stage 2 behavior as a bug. Reframe. The code faithfully implements the protocol §3.3 describes. The accurate statement: the paper used a stated but non-standard protocol that inflates P/R relative to one-to-one matching, and the Stage 1 evaluator inconsistently implemented a stricter rule, making the paper's S1-vs-S2 comparison mixed-protocol. The word “bug” belongs to the inconsistency, not to Stage 2 alone.

One genuinely defensible reading, so the erratum should say it: the paper's recall is a coverage metric — “what fraction of ramps had a detection within 90 px” — which is a legitimate question to ask. The problem is pairing a coverage-style recall with a detection-style precision and presenting both as standard P/R; and for the counting applications the paper motivates (city-scale audits), one-to-one is the honest protocol: a detector that returns one point for a dual-ramp corner has found one ramp, not two.

Provenance — Paper text: published PDF, §3.3/§3.4/§4.3. Code: git show v1.0-iccv2025 (evaluators quoted at exact tag). Aggregate metrics: Hyak klone re-eval, 2026-07-02, ckpt fingerprint b0c3ff7a10fc, results in stage_two/evaluation_results_new/. Real-example figures: generated locally 2026-07-02 — released HF weights (safetensors sha256 b4122c25…), CPU inference on 5 single panoramas fetched from the GSV tile endpoint the repo itself uses (minor JPEG differences vs. the HF dataset copies possible; illustration only, not an evaluation). Matcher traces: verbatim port of the tagged loop. This report was produced during the July 2026 post-release verification and accompanies GitHub issue #9; corrected aggregate results live in stage_two/evaluation_results_new/.
